description: The provided image is irrelevant to the research content of the paper focusing on Fe35MnxAl0.1C low-density steel. It displays a software function button with the readable text "Check for updates", accompanied by a logo consisting of a partial blue-yellow circular ring and a red bookmark-shaped pattern inside. No valid information related to the material composition, microstructure characterization, mechanical property testing, or deformation mechanism analysis of the studied low-density steels can be extracted from this image. It is inferred that the image may be incorrectly uploaded or a placeholder for loading failure, and all relevant research information needs to be obtained from the accompanying text extraction results.

This figure presents the microstructures of solution-treated Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C low-density steels with Al contents of 4 wt.% (4Al), 6 wt.% (6Al), 8 wt.% (8Al) and 10 wt.% (10Al). (a-d) are EBSD phase maps where red represents FCC austenite (γ) and blue represents BCC ferrite (α). With increasing Al content, the austenite fraction decreases, transforming from single austenite (4Al), austenite-based duplex (6Al), ferrite-based duplex (8Al) to nearly full ferrite (10Al). (e-h) are selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns along [100]γ zone axis, showing γ diffraction spots and L1₂-type short-range ordering superlattice spots whose intensity decreases with higher Al content. (i,k,m) are SAED patterns along [110]α zone axis, confirming coexistence of α, DO₃ and B2 ordered domains in ferrite. (j,l,n) are TEM dark-field images of DO₃ domains, with domain size increasing from ~2.0 nm (6Al), ~2.1 nm (8Al) to ~19 nm (10Al). (o) shows acicular 18R martensite in 10Al steel, and (p) EDS mappings reveal 18R is enriched in Mn and C while depleted in Al. The results demonstrate that Al content significantly regulates the phase composition, ordered structure and precipitation behavior of the test steels.

This figure consists of two subfigures for Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C low-density steels with Al content of 4, 6, 8, 10 wt% (marked as 4Al, 6Al, 8Al, 10Al, all processed via homogenization annealing, hot forging, 1050 °C solution treatment and oil quenching). Subfigure (a) presents XRD patterns in the 2θ range of 40° to 100°, quantifying phase fractions: 4Al steel is 100% face-centered cubic austenite (γ), 6Al steel contains 3.0% body-centered cubic ferrite (α) and 97.0% γ, 8Al steel has 75.7% α and 24.3% γ, 10Al steel contains 98.1% α and 1.9% γ, revealing that α phase fraction rises with increasing Al content while γ fraction reduces. Subfigure (b) shows microhardness (HV) of γ and α phases as a function of Al content: both phases show increasing hardness with Al content rising from 4 wt% to 10 wt%, and α phase always has significantly higher hardness than γ, confirming α acts as the strengthening phase in the steels. This figure demonstrates that Al content regulates phase constitution of the low-density steels, and ferrite strengthening contributes to improved mechanical performance.

This figure presents the mechanical properties of Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C low-density steels with Al content of 4, 6, 8, 10 wt% (denoted as 4Al, 6Al, 8Al, 10Al respectively). Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves, (b) plots yield strength (YS), ultimate tensile strength (UTS), uniform elongation (UEL), and total elongation (TEL) as functions of Al content, (c) displays true stress-strain curves, (d) illustrates strain hardening rate versus true strain curves, and (e) presents microhardness evolution with true strain for 4Al steel, austenite (γ) and ferrite (α) phases in 8Al steel. As Al content increases from 4 wt% to 8 wt%, YS and UTS increase significantly while elongation decreases slightly, showing a good strength-ductility balance for 8Al steel; 10Al steel exhibits brittle fracture with extremely low elongation (~4.6%), which is attributed to coarsened DO3 ordered domains and 18R martensite formation. 8Al steel shows multi-stage strain hardening behavior, and the ferrite phase in 8Al presents initial softening followed by hardening during deformation. The results demonstrate that the duplex microstructure of 8Al steel delivers optimal comprehensive mechanical performance.

This group of 9 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images paired with corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns demonstrates the post-tensile deformation microstructures of Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C low-density steels with Al content of 4, 6, 8, 10 wt% (marked as 4Al, 6Al, 8Al, 10Al steels). Subfigures (a), (b), (d), (e), (g) characterize the FCC austenite (γ) phase, while (c), (f), (h), (i) characterize the BCC ferrite (α) phase, all with a 500 nm scale bar. Deformed austenite mainly presents dislocation cell structures across all Al contents, with a small amount of deformation twins observed in 8Al austenite and planar slip features in 10Al austenite. Ferrite exhibits unusual planar slip characteristics for BCC structure, and increasing Al content promotes this planar slip behavior. Only a few long straight screw dislocations are activated in 10Al ferrite, inducing its brittleness. This figure confirms the distinct deformation mechanisms of austenite and ferrite, and the Al-dependent deformation behavior of the duplex low-density steels.

This set of TEM micrographs coupled with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns illustrates the microstructure evolution of austenite (γ, FCC phase) and ferrite (α, BCC phase) in Fe-35Mn-8Al-0.1C (8Al) ferrite-based duplex low-density steel at engineering strains of 3%, 10%, 20%, and 28%. At 3% strain, planar dislocation slip along (1̄11) plane and cross-slip occur in γ, while α shows slip bands along (11̄2) plane with activated edge, mixed, and screw dislocations, and SAED confirms DO3 ordered domains in α. As strain increases to 10%, cross-slip in γ becomes more pronounced, and slip bands in α evolve further. At 20% strain, dislocation cell blocks and dislocation cells form in γ, while slip bands in α are refined. At 28% strain, γ exhibits refined dislocation substructures and deformation twins, and α shows further refined slip bands with obvious dislocation cross-slip. This figure reveals the progressive deformation mechanism of the duplex steel, supporting that coordinated deformation of dual phases enhances mechanical properties.

This line chart statistically presents the average slip band (SB) spacing in ferrite (α_SB, black line), average slip band spacing plus dislocation cell block (DCB) size in austenite (γ_SB+DCB, red line), and average dislocation cell (DC) size in austenite (γ_DC, blue line) of Fe-35Mn-8Al-0.1C (8Al) low-density steel as a function of engineering strain (x-axis, range 0.00 to 0.30), with y-axis representing the size/spacing in nanometers (0 to 1400 nm). All three measured parameters show a monotonic decreasing trend with increasing engineering strain: α_SB reduces from ~760 nm at ~0.03 strain to ~80 nm at 0.28 strain, γ_SB+DCB decreases from ~900 nm at ~0.03 strain to ~220 nm at 0.28 strain, and γ_DC drops from ~260 nm at 0.20 strain to ~200 nm at 0.28 strain, with error bars indicating statistical variation of the measurements. The data from this figure are used for flow stress calculation of the 8Al steel, supporting the conclusion that continuous refinement of deformation microstructures (slip bands, dislocation cell blocks, dislocation cells) during tensile deformation contributes to the excellent strain hardening capability of the duplex low-density steel.

This set of micrographs presents the dislocation morphologies in ferrite phase of Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C low-density steels (x=10 wt% as 10Al steel, x=8 wt% as 8Al steel) after 3% tensile deformation, acquired via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with two-beam diffraction mode. Subfigures (a)-(f) correspond to 10Al steel, while (g)-(l) correspond to 8Al steel. Each subfigure includes a selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern at the upper right, indicating the operating diffraction vector g and [111] zone axis, with a 500 nm scale bar for the TEM view. g·b analysis confirms the Burgers vector of dislocations in ferrite of both steels is a/2<111>. For 10Al steel, ferrite dislocations are dominated by long straight screw dislocations with non-uniform distribution. For 8Al duplex ferrite-austenite steel, screw, edge, and mixed dislocations all exist with planar slip feature, and edge dislocations are preferentially activated at α/γ phase boundaries. These results demonstrate that Al content and duplex microstructure significantly regulate ferrite dislocation behavior during deformation, supporting the mechanism that duplex microstructure enhances mechanical properties of the low-density steel.

This set of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images characterize planar slip of dislocations in ferrite of Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C low-density steels with 10 wt% Al (10Al) and 8 wt% Al (8Al). Subfigures (a-c) correspond to 10Al steel: (a) is low-magnification bright-field TEM showing dislocation arrays, (b) is magnified view of (a) identifying screw dislocations and dislocation pairing, (c) is dark-field TEM acquired using (1-1-1) DO3 superlattice reflection, confirming large DO3 ordered domains in 10Al steel cannot be fully sheared by dislocations. Subfigures (d-i) correspond to 8Al steel: (d) is low-magnification bright-field TEM showing planar slip bands, (e) is magnified view of (d) identifying edge, screw, mixed dislocations and dislocation pairing, (f) is DO3 superlattice dark-field TEM confirming small DO3 ordered domains in 8Al steel can be fully sheared. (g) is high-resolution TEM along [100] zone axis, (h) and (i) are inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) images of positions 1 and 2 in (g) along (01-1) and (0-11) planes respectively, revealing dislocation dipoles shear the DO3 superlattice along dislocation lines and slip planes. This figure supports that higher Al content leads to larger DO3 ordered domains and higher anti-phase boundary energy, causing more significant dislocation pairing in 10Al steel, while smaller DO3 domains in 8Al steel are easier to shear, explaining their distinct mechanical performance differences.

This figure presents the relationship between deformation microstructure and flow stress of Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C low-density steels. Subplot (a) shows the linear fitting of (τ-τ₀)/G/10⁻³ versus b/D/10⁻³ for 4Al austenitic steel, based on average slip band spacing, dislocation cell block size and dislocation cell size, obtaining similitude constants K_DC=2.02 and K_SB+DCB=2.03, which are consistent with reported K values for austenitic stainless steels. Subplot (b) compares measured true stress (black line) and calculated stress (red line, decomposed into σ₀, σ_α(ε) and σ_λ(ε) representing intrinsic strength, dislocation/slip band strengthening and other strengthening contributions respectively) of 8Al steel at different true strains, with good agreement between experimental and calculated results. This figure validates that the proposed flow stress model accurately predicts mechanical properties of the studied low-density steels, and confirms that microstructure refinement is a key strengthening mechanism.
